MATERIAL KNOWLEDGE

Machined Material Performance Parameter Comparison: Material Selection and Machining Process Reference Guide

Mechanical properties, thermal conductivity, hardness, corrosion resistance, and machinability directly determine part service life, machining cost, and processing approach. This guide summarizes core comparison data for carbon steel, stainless steel, aluminum alloys, titanium alloys, and common engineering plastics to provide a practical reference for component material selection.

Material Knowledge Material Selection CNC Machining Engineering Plastics
SELECTION OVERVIEW

Material parameters are the foundation of machining process planning

In CNC machining, sheet metal, and 3D printing scenarios, material parameters are the basis for process planning. Density, strength, thermal conductivity, work hardening, corrosion resistance, and machining difficulty jointly affect part performance, machining cost, and final delivery lead time.

This article is for R&D engineers, structural designers, procurement staff, and manufacturing process engineers
01 Metal Material Comparison 02 Selection Parameter Table 03 Engineering Plastic Characteristics 04 Six Selection Criteria
01
METAL MATERIALS

Core parameter comparison of metal materials

Common machined metals differ significantly in density, thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, and machinability. Choosing different materials for the same part structure leads to different machining difficulty, surface finishing requirements, cost levels, and delivery outcomes.

Carbon Steel

Q235 / 45# Steel

Carbon steel has a density of about 7.85 g/cm³, offers strong value, and is relatively easy to machine, making it suitable for general structural supports. Quenched-and-tempered 45# steel provides higher strength and is commonly used for shafts and conventional mechanical parts.

Key Advantages
Lower cost and good machinability
Main Limitations
Weaker corrosion resistance
Surface Finishing
Black oxide, galvanizing, and other rust-prevention treatments
Typical Applications
Supports, shafts, and general structural parts

Aluminum Alloy

6061 / 7075

Aluminum alloy has a density of about 2.7 g/cm³, strong lightweight advantages, and high thermal conductivity. It machines smoothly with relatively low tool wear and is commonly used for automation equipment and new energy enclosures.

Key Advantages
Light weight, good heat dissipation, high machining efficiency
Main Limitations
Lower rigidity and more noticeable thermal expansion
Machining Notes
Thin-wall parts require controlled fixturing and machining deformation
Typical Applications
Equipment housings, modules, heat dissipation parts, and brackets

Stainless Steel

304 / 316

Stainless steel offers excellent corrosion resistance, with 316 suitable for humid and some acid/alkali environments. However, stainless steel has poor thermal conductivity, obvious work hardening, and a tendency to built-up edge during cutting.

Key Advantages
Good corrosion resistance and structural rigidity
Main Limitations
Poor thermal conductivity and significant work hardening
Machining Efficiency
About 30% to 50% of aluminum alloy efficiency
Cost Profile
Machining cost is usually higher than aluminum alloy

Titanium Alloy

TC4

Titanium alloy offers high specific strength and outstanding corrosion resistance, commonly used for medical implants and aerospace structural parts. Because of its low thermal conductivity, cutting heat concentrates at the tool tip, making it a typical difficult-to-machine material.

Key Advantages
High specific strength and outstanding corrosion resistance
Main Limitations
Concentrated cutting heat and higher tool wear
Machining Requirements
Requires dedicated tooling, cooling, and cutting parameters
Typical Applications
Medical, aerospace, and high-end corrosion-resistant parts
02
PARAMETER COMPARISON

Common machined material selection reference

The table below supports early-stage project screening. Specific grades still require further evaluation based on part loads, operating temperature, corrosion environment, dimensional tolerances, and budget.

Material Key Characteristics Corrosion Performance Machining Characteristics Typical Application Areas
Carbon Steel Q235 / 45# Balanced strength and cost with broad applicability Weak; rust-prevention treatment is usually required Lower machining difficulty and controllable cost Structural supports and shaft parts
Aluminum Alloy 6061 / 7075 Lightweight, thermally conductive, and efficient to machine Can be improved through anodizing and similar treatments Smooth cutting with relatively low tool wear Automation components and new energy enclosures
Stainless Steel 304 / 316 Balanced rigidity and corrosion resistance Excellent; 316 suits harsher environments Obvious work hardening, built-up edge, and lower efficiency Valve bodies, fasteners, and corrosion-resistant components
Titanium Alloy TC4 High specific strength with balanced weight and performance Excellent Difficult to machine; higher tooling and cooling requirements Medical, aerospace, and high-end precision parts
03
ENGINEERING PLASTICS

Performance and machining characteristics of engineering plastics

Engineering plastics are lightweight and offer insulation, wear resistance, or self-lubrication, commonly used for gears, sliders, insulating structures, and non-metal mechanical parts. Different plastics vary significantly in temperature resistance, moisture absorption, and dimensional stability.

PEEK

Offers high temperature resistance, high strength, and wear resistance, suitable for parts with demanding temperature and mechanical performance requirements.

POM

Good self-lubrication, commonly used for gears, sliding components, and low-friction mechanical structures.

Nylon

Good toughness and impact resistance at relatively lower cost, suitable for conventional non-metal structural parts.

Engineering Plastic Machining Notes

Plastic parts can soften or melt under cutting temperature. Spindle speed, tool sharpness, fixturing method, and cooling strategy must be adjusted for each material.

04
SELECTION FACTORS

Material selection requires attention to six criteria at once

  1. 01

    Density

    Density directly affects part weight and is the first parameter to compare in lightweight structural design.

  2. 02

    Tensile Strength

    Determine whether a material meets structural strength requirements based on part loading, load type, and safety factor.

  3. 03

    Thermal Conductivity

    Thermal conductivity affects both in-service heat dissipation and how cutting heat is transferred during machining.

  4. 04

    Work Hardening

    The more pronounced the work hardening, the greater the tool wear and the lower the continuous machining efficiency.

  5. 05

    Corrosion Resistance

    Evaluate based on actual conditions such as humidity, salt spray, acid/alkali exposure, and outdoor environments.

  6. 06

    Machining Difficulty

    Machining difficulty affects machine time, tool consumption, quoting level, and delivery lead time.

MATERIAL SELECTION

Material selection directly affects quoting, lead time, and finished-part quality

The same part made from different materials can show significant differences in machining quote, delivery lead time, and finished quality. Early in the project, define the operating environment, load requirements, weight limits, and target cost before matching material grades and machining processes.

ProMakeHub has compiled material parameters and machining experience for common metals and engineering plastics and can recommend material grades and processing methods based on part operating conditions.

Material Reference

Kunshan Meifuxin Electromechanical, Dalian Fuhong Machinery material articles, and Renrenwenku material parameter handbooks.
